behaviors.description: Products marked as out-of-stock are excluded from the recommendation results regardless of their relevance score.
behaviors.justification: A pure in-process mock of products would hide integration failures: the filter may not be applied at all, applied at the wrong pipeline stage, or never receive data from the actual product catalog. Must test the full recommendation pipeline with mixed stock states.
behaviors.test_description: Populate the product catalog with 20 items: 10 in-stock with relevance scores 10–50, and 10 out-of-stock with higher scores 60–100. Call the recommendation endpoint for a user and verify no out-of-stock products appear in results, regardless of score.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test requires integrated product retrieval, filtering, and ranking layers—cannot verify by testing filter logic in isolation.

behaviors.description: The user preference filter correctly excludes product categories the user has explicitly blocked in their settings.
behaviors.justification: Mocking user settings would hide failures in loading preferences from storage, validating them, or integrating them into the filtering pipeline. Real user state and populated catalog required.
behaviors.test_description: Create a user with blocked categories [electronics, books]. Populate the catalog with items across electronics, books, clothing, and home. Call the recommendation endpoint and verify no blocked-category products appear.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test must verify the entire preferences-load-and-filter chain, not just the filter logic.

behaviors.description: The recommendation algorithm ranks products that match the user's recent browsing patterns higher than products with no browsing signal.
behaviors.justification: Mocking browsing history masks failures in data retrieval, recency validation, and integration with ranking. Real browsing data must flow through the system to verify the algorithm actually receives and uses it.
behaviors.test_description: Create a user with browsing history in the sports category only. Populate the catalog with 10 sports and 10 home products. Call the recommendation endpoint and verify sports products have a higher average rank position than home products.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Testing ranking requires verified data retrieval and algorithmic application, not just ranking formula correctness in isolation.

behaviors.description: Pagination returns the correct page of results, with stable ordering — requesting page 2 twice returns identical results if the underlying data hasn't changed.
behaviors.justification: Stable ordering across repeated requests depends on deterministic database queries or persistent data structures. In-process mocks cannot verify database cursor behavior or ordering guarantees.
behaviors.test_description: Populate the catalog with 150 products and request page 2 twice (assuming 10 results per page). Verify both responses contain identical products in the same order.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Stable ordering requires testing against actual data persistence and query ordering, not pagination logic alone.

behaviors.description: The recommendation algorithm surfaces at least one product from a category the user hasn't browsed before (serendipity requirement), avoiding a filter bubble.
behaviors.justification: Mocking categories masks failures where serendipity logic is disconnected from ranking, not triggered for cold categories, or fails with realistic category distributions. Real diverse product data required.
behaviors.test_description: Create a user with browsing history in category_A only. Populate the catalog with products across categories A–E. Request 10 recommendations and verify at least 1 product from categories B–E appears.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Serendipity must be tested as an integrated behavior—algorithm logic alone doesn't guarantee it reaches the final recommendation list.

behaviors.description: A new user with no purchase or browsing history receives recommendations based on overall popularity rather than an empty result set.
behaviors.justification: Mocking popularity data hides failures in cold-start path selection, popularity data loading, or its application when user history is absent. Full pipeline must execute.
behaviors.test_description: Create a new user with no history. Populate the catalog with products and realistic popularity scores. Call the recommendation endpoint and verify non-empty results sorted by popularity.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Cold-start behavior must be tested end-to-end to verify popularity fallback is actually triggered and applied.

behaviors.description: Click tracking records the correct product ID, position in the list, and timestamp for each recommendation the user interacts with.
behaviors.justification: Mocking database writes always succeeds, hiding data loss, type mismatches, incomplete fields, or serialization bugs. Real persistence layer must be verified.
behaviors.test_description: Call the recommendation endpoint, receive results with product IDs and positions. Simulate a click on position 3. Query the database and verify a click record exists with correct product_id, position=3, and valid timestamp.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Click tracking is only verified by testing actual database persistence, not mocked writes.

behaviors.description: The response time for generating recommendations stays under 200ms for a catalog of 100,000 products.
behaviors.justification: Performance requirements cannot be tested with mocks or small datasets. Real data volume (100,000 items), actual algorithm, and realistic data access patterns required.
behaviors.test_description: Populate the catalog with 100,000 products and realistic attributes. For a user with browsing history, call the recommendation endpoint and measure response time. Verify it is under 200ms.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Performance testing requires full algorithmic stack with real data volume—mocks eliminate the computational work that would cause slowness.

behaviors.description: When a recommended product is purchased by the user, the recommendation algorithm deprioritizes it in subsequent requests.
behaviors.justification: Mocking purchase events hides failures in purchase capture, persistence, and integration with ranking. Real purchase state must feed back into the recommendation pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Create a user with browsing history, get recommendations, and note the product at position 1. Simulate a purchase of that product. Get new recommendations for the same user and verify the purchased product no longer appears in the top 5.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Deprioritization requires testing the full purchase-tracking and re-ranking cycle, not just ranking logic.

behaviors.description: The recommendation results are deterministic for the same user state — requesting recommendations twice with no intervening user activity returns the same ranked list.
behaviors.justification: Mocking the recommendation logic always produces consistent results, masking non-determinism at any layer of the system (randomization, caching issues, concurrency). Full endpoint must be called twice to verify all components.
behaviors.test_description: Create a fixed user state (specific browsing history, preferences). Call the recommendation endpoint twice with no intervening user activity. Verify both responses contain identical products in the same order.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Determinism across the full system can only be verified by calling the endpoint multiple times and comparing actual results.
